topic: What 4,000 tests and zero staging environment taught us about production confidence
format: Describe a technical problem that took longer than it should have — what made it hard, what fixed it, what you'd do differently
post:
A bug in our WPS payroll logic took me six hours to find. The fix was four lines.

What made it hard wasn't the code. It was that I didn't trust the tests — so I kept looking past them.

We had no staging environment when we shipped the first version of SmartPRO Hub. Oman's first multi-tenant HR intelligence platform, handling WPS payroll with direct bank integration and Ministry of Labour compliance, going straight to production.

That sounds reckless. Honestly, it was — right up until it wasn't.

What kept us honest was 4,000+ automated tests across tRPC endpoints, Drizzle ORM mutations, and multi-tenant isolation logic. End-of-service gratuity calculations. Work permit expiry edge cases. Payroll cutoff timing for bank files. When there's no staging to hide in, those tests have to actually mean something. Not coverage numbers — real assertions against the scenarios your users will hit on a bad Tuesday.

What shifted our confidence wasn't the deployment pipeline. It was writing tests that made us a little uncomfortable. Tests that assumed the worst from users. Tests where we already knew the right answer before we ran them, and ran them anyway.

The honest tradeoff: it's slower. Some features took twice as long to ship. We made that call deliberately and I'd make it again. We've never had a payroll calculation wrong in production. On a platform touching people's salaries and government compliance, that's not a KPI — it's the whole point.

If you're on a lean team in a market where one bad payroll run costs you everything, write the test you're most afraid to fail. Write it first.

See SmartPRO Hub in action — book a free 30-minute demo: https://thesmartpro.io/book?type=demo&utm_source=linkedin&utm_medium=social&utm_campaign=tech
